does: This paper presents a novel adversarial attack method against in-context learning in large language models, where an attacker acting as a model publisher injects imperceptible adversarial suffixes into the demonstration examples within prompts. The attack uses gradient-based prompt search to craft these adversarial suffixes that cause the model to generate target outputs or elicit harmful responses. The work is evaluated on both classification tasks and jailbreak scenarios, demonstrating high transferability across different model architectures. Additionally, the paper proposes defense mechanisms based on augmenting prompts with clean demonstration examples, showing these defenses can meaningfully improve robustness against such attacks without significantly degrading task performance.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature examining adversarial robustness of LLMs, extending beyond previous prompt injection and adversarial attack methods by specifically targeting the in-context learning paradigm. It connects to earlier research on prompt-based attacks that either require visible triggers or are easy to detect, as well as broader work on transferable adversarial examples in deep learning. The paper also engages with concurrent research on jailbreaking LLMs and the emerging understanding of how models process demonstration examples in few-shot learning scenarios. The gradient-based optimization approach draws from established adversarial example generation techniques adapted to the discrete token space of language models.
enables: This work opens directions for developing more robust in-context learning procedures, particularly around careful curation and verification of demonstration examples in deployed systems. It motivates future research into detecting subtle adversarial modifications in prompts and developing certified defenses for ICL. The threat model also highlights security considerations for third-party demonstration repositories and model hosting services. Follow-up work could explore more sophisticated defenses leveraging uncertainty quantification or adversarial training on ICL, investigation of which architectural features make models vulnerable to such attacks, and deeper analysis of when and why adversarial suffixes in demonstrations transfer across models.
